name: intrinsic

members.name: ConvReLU1d
members.extended:
A thin `lucid.nn.Sequential` of `[conv, relu]` emitted by
`lucid.quantization.fuse_modules`. It runs as **plain float** — it carries no
observers and no fake-quant, and computing through it is identical to running the conv
then the ReLU. Its whole purpose is *structural*: by packaging the two ops as one unit,
it tells the quantization pipeline to place a **single** activation observer *after* the
ReLU rather than between the conv and the ReLU.

**Why fuse for quantization.** If an observer sat on the raw conv output it would
calibrate a symmetric range that includes values the ReLU is about to discard, wasting
half the int8 codes on negatives that never reach inference. Observing the *post*-ReLU
range instead lets the eventual quantized layer pick a grid over $[0, \max]$,
roughly doubling resolution. Fusion also drops the intermediate float tensor between
conv and ReLU, so the converted kernel does conv → ReLU → requantize in one step.

**Where it sits.** This marker is the hand-off point between float and quantized worlds.
`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` replaces it with a trainable
`lucid.nn.qat.ConvReLU1d` (weight + post-ReLU fake-quant under an STE), and
`lucid.quantization.convert` maps it — or the QAT layer it became — to a fused
inference `lucid.nn.quantized.ConvReLU1d`.

As a plain-float module it simply computes, with $\star$ the 1-D
cross-correlation:

$$
y = \operatorname{ReLU}(W \star x + b)
$$

Downstream, once an observer is attached, that fused output is fake-quantized under a
straight-through estimator — rounded in the forward pass, identity in the backward:

$$
\operatorname{fake\_quant}(t) = \bigl(
    \operatorname{clamp}(\operatorname{round}(t / s) + z,\ q_{\min},\ q_{\max})
    - z\bigr)\, s,
\qquad
\frac{\partial\, \operatorname{fake\_quant}(t)}{\partial t} = 1
$$

parameters.name: conv
parameters.description: The 1-D convolution to run first; kept as the `[0]` child of the sequential.

parameters.name: relu
parameters.description: The rectifier applied to the convolution output; the `[1]` child.

examples:
>>> import lucid, lucid.nn as nn
>>> import lucid.quantization as Q
>>> import lucid.nn.intrinsic as nni
>>> m = nn.Sequential(nn.Conv1d(3, 8, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU())
>>> fused = Q.fuse_modules(m, [["0", "1"]])
>>> isinstance(fused[0], nni.ConvReLU1d)     # [conv, relu] collapsed to one marker
True
>>> fused(lucid.randn(2, 3, 8)).shape         # runs as plain float
(2, 8, 8)
notes:
- **Structural tag only.** It carries no quantization state — no `weight_fake_quant`,
  no `activation_post_process`. Those are attached later by
  `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` (or by `lucid.quantization.prepare` for
  the post-training path).
- **Post-ReLU observer placement.** The single reason to fuse is so the downstream
  activation observer sees the non-negative post-ReLU range.
- **Both directions are wired for you.** `lucid.quantization.fuse_modules` emits
  this marker *in*; `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` /
  `lucid.quantization.convert` map it *out* to the QAT / quantized fused layer.

see_also.name: lucid.nn.qat.ConvReLU1d
see_also.description: The trainable QAT layer `prepare_qat` swaps this for.

see_also.name: lucid.nn.quantized.ConvReLU1d
see_also.description: The fused int8 inference conv `convert` maps this to.

see_also.name: lucid.quantization.fuse_modules_qat
see_also.description: Fuses straight to the QAT layer.

members.name: ConvReLU2d
members.extended:
A thin `lucid.nn.Sequential` of `[conv, relu]` (with any BatchNorm already
folded into the conv weight) emitted by `lucid.quantization.fuse_modules` — the
dominant fused pattern in quantized vision backbones. It runs as **plain float** — it
carries no observers and no fake-quant, and computing through it is identical to running
the conv then the ReLU. Its whole purpose is *structural*: by packaging the two ops as
one unit it tells the quantization pipeline to place a **single** activation observer
*after* the ReLU rather than between the conv and the ReLU.

**Why fuse for quantization.** If an observer sat on the raw conv output it would
calibrate a symmetric range that includes values the ReLU is about to discard, wasting
half the int8 codes on negatives that never reach inference. Observing the *post*-ReLU
range instead lets the eventual quantized layer pick a grid over $[0, \max]$,
roughly doubling resolution. Fusion also drops the intermediate float tensor between
conv and ReLU, so the converted kernel does conv → ReLU → requantize in one step.

**Where it sits.** This marker is the hand-off point between float and quantized worlds.
`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` replaces it with a trainable
`lucid.nn.qat.ConvReLU2d` (weight + post-ReLU fake-quant under an STE), and
`lucid.quantization.convert` maps it — or the QAT layer it became — to a fused
inference `lucid.nn.quantized.ConvReLU2d`.

As a plain-float module it simply computes, with $\star$ the 2-D
cross-correlation:

$$
y = \operatorname{ReLU}(W \star x + b)
$$

Downstream, once an observer is attached, that fused output is fake-quantized under a
straight-through estimator — rounded in the forward pass, identity in the backward:

$$
\operatorname{fake\_quant}(t) = \bigl(
    \operatorname{clamp}(\operatorname{round}(t / s) + z,\ q_{\min},\ q_{\max})
    - z\bigr)\, s,
\qquad
\frac{\partial\, \operatorname{fake\_quant}(t)}{\partial t} = 1
$$

parameters.name: conv
parameters.description: The 2-D convolution to run first (BatchNorm already folded in); the `[0]` child.

parameters.name: relu
parameters.description: The rectifier applied to the convolution output; the `[1]` child.

examples:
>>> import lucid, lucid.nn as nn
>>> import lucid.quantization as Q
>>> import lucid.nn.intrinsic as nni
>>> m = nn.Sequential(nn.Conv2d(3, 8, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU())
>>> fused = Q.fuse_modules(m, [["0", "1"]])
>>> isinstance(fused[0], nni.ConvReLU2d)     # [conv, relu] collapsed to one marker
True
>>> fused(lucid.randn(2, 3, 8, 8)).shape       # runs as plain float
(2, 8, 8, 8)
notes:
- **Structural tag only.** It carries no quantization state — no `weight_fake_quant`,
  no `activation_post_process`. Those are attached later by
  `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` (or by `lucid.quantization.prepare` for
  the post-training path).
- **Post-ReLU observer placement.** The single reason to fuse is so the downstream
  activation observer sees the non-negative post-ReLU range.
- **Both directions are wired for you.** `lucid.quantization.fuse_modules` emits
  this marker *in*; `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` /
  `lucid.quantization.convert` map it *out* to the QAT / quantized fused layer.

see_also.name: lucid.nn.qat.ConvReLU2d
see_also.description: The trainable QAT layer `prepare_qat` swaps this for.

see_also.name: lucid.nn.quantized.ConvReLU2d
see_also.description: The fused int8 inference conv `convert` maps this to.

see_also.name: lucid.quantization.fuse_modules_qat
see_also.description: Fuses straight to the QAT layer.

members.name: ConvReLU3d
members.extended:
A thin `lucid.nn.Sequential` of `[conv, relu]` emitted by
`lucid.quantization.fuse_modules`. It runs as **plain float** — it carries no
observers and no fake-quant, and computing through it is identical to running the conv
then the ReLU. Its whole purpose is *structural*: by packaging the two ops as one unit
it tells the quantization pipeline to place a **single** activation observer *after* the
ReLU rather than between the conv and the ReLU.

**Why fuse for quantization.** If an observer sat on the raw conv output it would

roughly doubling resolution. Fusion also drops the intermediate float tensor between

`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` replaces it with a trainable
`lucid.nn.qat.ConvReLU3d` (weight + post-ReLU fake-quant under an STE), and
`lucid.quantization.convert` maps it — or the QAT layer it became — to a fused
inference `lucid.nn.quantized.ConvReLU3d`.

As a plain-float module it simply computes, with $\star$ the 3-D
cross-correlation:

$$
y = \operatorname{ReLU}(W \star x + b)
$$

Downstream, once an observer is attached, that fused output is fake-quantized under a
straight-through estimator — rounded in the forward pass, identity in the backward:

$$
\operatorname{fake\_quant}(t) = \bigl(
    \operatorname{clamp}(\operatorname{round}(t / s) + z,\ q_{\min},\ q_{\max})
    - z\bigr)\, s,
\qquad
\frac{\partial\, \operatorname{fake\_quant}(t)}{\partial t} = 1
$$

parameters.name: conv
parameters.description: The 3-D convolution to run first; kept as the `[0]` child of the sequential.

parameters.name: relu
parameters.description: The rectifier applied to the convolution output; the `[1]` child.

examples:
>>> import lucid, lucid.nn as nn
>>> import lucid.quantization as Q
>>> import lucid.nn.intrinsic as nni

>>> fused = Q.fuse_modules(m, [["0", "1"]])
>>> isinstance(fused[0], nni.ConvReLU3d)     # [conv, relu] collapsed to one marker
True
>>> fused(lucid.randn(2, 3, 4, 4, 4)).shape    # runs as plain float
(2, 8, 4, 4, 4)

- **Structural tag only.** It carries no quantization state — no `weight_fake_quant`,
  no `activation_post_process`. Those are attached later by
  `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` (or by `lucid.quantization.prepare` for
  the post-training path).
- **Post-ReLU observer placement.** The single reason to fuse is so the downstream
  activation observer sees the non-negative post-ReLU range.
- **Both directions are wired for you.** `lucid.quantization.fuse_modules` emits
  this marker *in*; `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` /
  `lucid.quantization.convert` map it *out* to the QAT / quantized fused layer.

members.name: LinearReLU
members.extended:
A thin `lucid.nn.Sequential` of `[linear, relu]` emitted by
`lucid.quantization.fuse_modules`. It runs as **plain float** — it carries no
observers and no fake-quant, and computing through it is identical to running the linear
then the ReLU. Its whole purpose is *structural*: by packaging the two ops as one unit
it tells the quantization pipeline to place a **single** activation observer *after* the
ReLU rather than between the linear and the ReLU.

**Why fuse for quantization.** If an observer sat on the raw linear output it would
calibrate a symmetric range that includes values the ReLU is about to discard, wasting
half the int8 codes on negatives that never reach inference. Observing the *post*-ReLU
range instead lets the eventual quantized layer pick a grid over $[0, \max]$,
roughly doubling resolution. Fusion also drops the intermediate float tensor between
linear and ReLU, so the converted kernel does matmul → ReLU → requantize in one step.

**Where it sits.** This marker is the hand-off point between float and quantized worlds.
`lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` replaces it with a trainable
`lucid.nn.qat.LinearReLU` (weight + post-ReLU fake-quant under an STE), and
`lucid.quantization.convert` maps it — or the QAT layer it became — to a fused
inference `lucid.nn.quantized.LinearReLU`.

As a plain-float module it simply computes:

$$
y = \operatorname{ReLU}(W x^{\top} + b)
$$

Downstream, once an observer is attached, that fused output is fake-quantized under a
straight-through estimator — rounded in the forward pass, identity in the backward:

$$
\operatorname{fake\_quant}(t) = \bigl(
    \operatorname{clamp}(\operatorname{round}(t / s) + z,\ q_{\min},\ q_{\max})
    - z\bigr)\, s,
\qquad
\frac{\partial\, \operatorname{fake\_quant}(t)}{\partial t} = 1
$$

parameters.name: linear
parameters.description:
The linear (fully-connected) layer to run first; the `[0]` child of the
sequential.

parameters.name: relu
parameters.description: The rectifier applied to the linear output; the `[1]` child.

examples:
>>> import lucid, lucid.nn as nn
>>> import lucid.quantization as Q
>>> import lucid.nn.intrinsic as nni
>>> m = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(32, 16), nn.ReLU())
>>> fused = Q.fuse_modules(m, [["0", "1"]])
>>> isinstance(fused[0], nni.LinearReLU)     # [linear, relu] collapsed to one marker
True
>>> fused(lucid.randn(8, 32)).shape           # runs as plain float
(8, 16)
notes:
- **Structural tag only.** It carries no quantization state — no `weight_fake_quant`,
  no `activation_post_process`. Those are attached later by
  `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` (or by `lucid.quantization.prepare` for
  the post-training path).
- **Post-ReLU observer placement.** The single reason to fuse is so the downstream
  activation observer sees the non-negative post-ReLU range.
- **Both directions are wired for you.** `lucid.quantization.fuse_modules` emits
  this marker *in*; `lucid.quantization.prepare_qat` /
  `lucid.quantization.convert` map it *out* to the QAT / quantized fused layer.

see_also.name: lucid.nn.qat.LinearReLU
see_also.description: The trainable QAT layer `prepare_qat` swaps this for.

see_also.name: lucid.nn.quantized.LinearReLU
see_also.description: The fused int8 inference layer `convert` maps this to.

see_also.name: lucid.quantization.fuse_modules_qat
see_also.description: Fuses straight to the QAT layer.

extended:
Produced by `lucid.quantization.fuse_modules` and mapped to their
`nn.quantized` counterparts during `lucid.quantization.convert`.
